actions.thought: I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 7d5d, a suited one-gapper. In heads-up play, this is a respectable hand to play, especially with the advantage of position post-flop. With effective stacks at 100BB, I want to take the initiative rather than just limping. A min-raise to 200 is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the Big Blind, balances my opening range, and allows me to play a pot in position with a hand that has good playability (flush and straight potential).

I am in the Big Blind with 84o. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call the additional 100 chips, 84o is a very weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially when playing out of position. I will choose to fold and wait for a more advantageous hand.
AQo is a premium hand in heads-up play. I have the positional advantage on the button. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size to build the pot and exert pressure on the opponent.

I hold 5h 9d in the Big Blind facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. This is a weak offsuit hand (trash) that performs poorly out of position. While I get 2:1 pot odds, my hand equity is barely borderline against a wide range, and the poor playability makes it difficult to realize that equity. Defending here would likely result in losing more chips post-flop. Therefore, folding is the standard and prudent play.

In heads-up play, JTo is a solid hand to open from the Button (Small Blind). I have position and a hand with decent playability and high card strength. A standard min-raise puts pressure on the Big Blind while keeping the pot manageable relative to the stack depth (approx. 100 BBs).

I have A4o in the Big Blind. Pro raised to 2x (200) from the Small Blind. I need to call 100 more to see a flop. The pot is currently 300 (100 from my BB + 200 from Pro), so calling 100 gives me 3:1 pot odds. A4o has plenty of equity against a standard button opening range to justify a call. I will defend my blind and see the flop.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor nut flush draw on a Qh-8c-2c board. I am first to act against the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out here doesn't achieve much, as I'll likely only be called by better hands. I will check to the Button to see how they play the board, intending to evaluate based on their bet sizing or take a free card if they check back.

On a Flop of Qh 8c 2c, I hold Jh Td. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9) and one overcard to the second pair. Checking back is the preferred line here for several reasons: 1) It allows me to realize my equity for free, trying to hit the straight or a pair on the turn. 2) I avoid getting check-raised, which would force me to fold my equity. 3) My hand has marginal showdown value potential but cannot comfortably call a raise or fire three streets of bluffs. Controlling the pot size is optimal.

The turn 5s is a blank. I currently hold Ace-high with the nut flush draw. Pro checked back on the flop, which often signals a capped range or a desire to reach showdown cheaply with a marginal hand. I will check here to see if I can get a free card to hit my flush or to evaluate Pro's strength if they decide to bet. Checking also keeps the pot small while I'm still drawing.

On the Turn, the board is Qh 8c 2c 5s. The 5s is a relative brick. I checked back the flop with my gutshot straight draw (J-T needing a 9) to realize equity, and the situation remains similar. The opponent checked to me again. Betting here as a delayed c-bet is an option to fold out better high cards or small pairs, but my hand still has decent equity (4 outs to the nuts) that I want to realize for free. If I bet and get check-raised, I lose that equity. Checking back allows me to see the river for free, potentially hit my straight, or bluff the river if the opponent shows extreme weakness. Pot control is preferred with a marginal draw.

I have Ace-high (Ac 4h) on a board of Qh-8c-2c-5s-7h. The action has been checked through on both the flop and the turn after a pre-flop raise from Pro. This passive line from Pro suggests they likely have a marginal hand or a missed draw. My Ace-high has a decent amount of showdown value in a small, checked-down pot. Betting doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to fold out a pair and will only get called by better hands. I will check and hope to win at showdown against other missed draws or lower Ace-high hands.

On the River, the board is Qh 8c 2c 5s 7h. I hold Jack-high (Jh Td) which has virtually no showdown value, as I lose to Ace-high, King-high, and any pair. The opponent (Flash) has shown extreme passivity by checking to me on the Flop, Turn, and now River. This line strongly suggests a capped, weak range consisting of missed draws, Ace-high/King-high types, or perhaps a very weak bottom pair (2x or 5x). If I check back, I almost certainly lose the pot to his high cards. By betting, I can credibly represent a thin value hand (like an 8, a 7, or a small pocket pair) that checked back earlier for pot control and is now betting for value against a weak range. A bet of roughly half-pot (220 into 400) generates enough fold equity to make A-high and K-high fold, turning a likely loss into a win.

I checked the river with the intention of going to showdown with my Ace-high, as the passive line taken by Pro on the flop and turn suggested a weak or marginal range. However, Pro has now led out for 220 into a 400 pot (a 55% pot bet). While I am getting roughly 3.8:1 odds on a call, Ace-high is a very weak bluff-catcher. Pro's line often represents thin value, such as a pair of eights or a pair of sevens that improved on the river, or a hand like pocket sixes. While it is possible Pro is bluffing with a missed club draw or a hand like JT, Ace-high loses to any pair. In the early stages of a 150-hand tournament, I prefer to fold and preserve my stack rather than making a high-variance call with a weak hand.

I am on the button with King-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Standard strategy dictates an open-raise to build the pot and exert pressure on the big blind. I will raise to 300, a standard 3x sizing.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jh Jc), a premium pair, in the Big Blind. The opponent raised to 300 (3BB) from the Button. In heads-up play, the button opens a very wide range of hands. JJ is significantly ahead of this range. While calling is an option to trap, 3-betting for value is generally preferred to charge weaker pairs, Ax hands, and broadway cards, as well as to define the pot size before the flop. Raising to 1000 (approx 3.3x their open) applies pressure and builds the pot while I have a strong equity advantage.

I am holding KQo on the button, which is a top-tier hand in heads-up play. Pro has 3-bet my open to 1000. Folding would be way too conservative here given the strength of my hand. While I could 4-bet, calling allows me to play a pot in position and see a flop, keeping Pro's range wider and potentially allowing me to outplay him post-flop. The price to call is 700 more into a pot that will be 2000, giving me good odds.

I am out of position in a 3-bet pot with a stack-to-pot ratio of roughly 4.4. The board (Qd-Td-2h) hits the opponent's calling range fairly well, as they likely have Qx broadways (AQs, KQs, QJs), sets (QQ, TT), or flush draws. While I have an overpair to the Ten and beat hands like AT or lower pocket pairs, my Pocket Jacks act effectively as second pair here. Betting creates a situation where I likely only get called by better hands (Qx) or strong draws, while folding out hands I dominate. Checking protects my range, allows me to control the size of the pot, and lets me bluff-catch if the opponent bets with a draw or a weaker ten. I want to realize my equity and get to showdown without bloating the pot with a marginal hand on this texture.

I have top pair with a strong King kicker on a board that contains both flush and straight draws. Pro 3-bet pre-flop but has checked the flop. While this could be a trap with AA, KK, or a set, I need to bet for value against draws (like flush draws or KJ) and middle pairs (like TT or JJ). A bet of 1000, which is half the pot, is a standard sizing to extract value and protect my hand while keeping the pot manageable if I am behind.

I hold Pocket Jacks (Jh Jc) on a board of Qd-Td-2h. After 3-betting pre-flop and checking the flop, the opponent has bet 1000 into a pot of 2000. The board connects well with the opponent's calling range (Qx, Sets, Flush Draws, Straight Draws), but in a heads-up scenario, their range also includes many bluffs and semi-bluffs (Flush draws, KJ, J9). Folding is too conservative as my hand beats all draws and weaker pairs (Tx). Raising would likely only get called by better hands (Qx+) and fold out bluffs. Calling is the optimal line to keep the opponent's bluffs in the range and control the pot size while bluff-catching. The price (1000 to win 3000) is favorable.

The Turn card is the Jack of Diamonds (Jd), giving me a Set of Jacks. However, the board is now extremely coordinated and dangerous: Qd-Td-Jd presents a possible completed flush and multiple straight possibilities (any King makes a straight). While I have a very strong hand in absolute terms, relatively, I could be drawing to a full house against a flush or straight. Leading out here (donk betting) is ill-advised because worse hands will likely fold, and better hands (flushes, straights) will raise or call, putting me in a difficult spot. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size, induce bluffs from the opponent who showed aggression on the flop, and realize my equity. If the opponent bets, I have the odds to call looking to pair the board on the river.

The Jack of Diamonds is a highly coordinated card that completes many possible draws including flushes and straights (AK, K9). While I have top pair and an open-ended straight draw, the board is now very dangerous. Pro check-called the flop and has checked the turn again. Checking behind here allows me to control the size of the pot, realize my equity, and see the river for free. I still have significant showdown value, but betting into a board where I could easily be beat by a flush or a straight is risky.

I hold a Full House (Jacks full of Deuces) on a board of Td-2h-Qd-Jd-2s. The opponent bet the flop but checked back the turn. This line typically indicates a capped range, often consisting of Top Pair (Qx), a Flush/Straight that got scared of the board pairing/texture, or a give-up. The river 2s is a safe card for me that pairs the board. If I check, the opponent likely checks back hands like Qx or Straights/Flushes fearing a boat. To extract value, I should lead out. A bet of 1800 (slightly less than half pot) targets Top Pair (AQ, KQ) and cautiously played Flushes or Straights, offering them a price they might call, whereas a check risks missing value from these showdown-value hands.

I have top pair with a King kicker, but the board is extremely coordinated. The Jack of Diamonds on the turn completed a potential straight for any Ace-King or King-9 combination, and the three diamonds on the board make a flush a possibility for any two diamonds in the opponent's range. Pro's 3-bet pre-flop suggests a range of high cards and pairs like AA, KK, JJ, TT, AK, and AQ, almost all of which currently beat my hand. My check on the turn for pot control likely signaled a capped range, and Pro's 1800 lead on the river is a substantial bet into a 4000-chip pot. I am essentially only beating a total bluff or a lower pair turned into a bluff, which is unlikely given the 3-bet history. To preserve my stack for better opportunities, I will fold.

In a Heads-Up match with 77BB effective stacks, I am on the Button (SB) with King-Deuce offsuit. While heads-up strategy requires a wide opening range (often 80%+), K2o represents the bottom of the King range with poor post-flop playability and reverse implied odds. I have been aggressive in previous hands (raising 57s, TJs, 3-betting JJ), establishing a strong image. Folding this marginal hand is a disciplined play to maintain a balanced frequency and avoid bleeding chips in a low-EV spot, specifically when I hold a chip lead and can wait for better opportunities.

I am on the button with 3h 4c, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have position, this hand has very little playability and equity against a standard big blind defending range. Given that the stack sizes are relatively deep (nearly 80 BBs), there's no need to take unnecessary risks with junk hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to exploit my opponent.

I am on the Button with 5s2s. This is a very weak hand, even in heads-up play. While suited, it is a low gapper with poor equity against any calling range. I have been aggressive on the button previously (raising 57s and TJo), but I also folded K2o. Folding here is standard to maintain a solid range and not over-bluff with the bottom of my holdings.

I am on the button with a premium starting hand, King-Queen suited. Standard heads-up strategy dictates a raise to build the pot and take control of the hand. I have previously raised to 300 in similar positions and will maintain that sizing to keep my range balanced while extracting value from a strong holding.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 7c against a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button. The pot odds require 33% equity to call (200 to win 600). While 87o has raw equity of roughly 40-45% against a wide heads-up opening range, the playability is poor out of position, making it difficult to realize that equity. Against a 3bb sizing rather than a min-raise, the price is steeper, and 87o falls into the folding range to avoid bleeding chips in marginal spots early in the match. I will fold.

In Heads-Up play, J8o is a playable hand from the Button (SB), ranking around the top 53% of hands. While not premium, it benefits significantly from position post-flop. I have folded my last two buttons (Hands 5 and 7), so I need to widen my opening range to prevent the opponent from exploiting my recent passivity. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard size to steal the blind or build a pot in position without over-committing with a marginal hand. Limping is an alternative, but raising puts more immediate pressure on the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 4d and facing a min-raise from the button. The pot odds are 3:1 (100 to call into a 300 pot), which is very favorable. Although the hand is weak, its suitedness provides some post-flop playability in a heads-up situation. Calling is the standard play here to see a flop and realize my equity without bloating the pot out of position with a marginal hand.

I have flopped top pair with 9d 4d on a 6c 8d 9h board. While I have top pair, the board is very connected and dangerous, offering many straight possibilities (any 7 or 5-7, 7-10). My kicker (4) is very weak, meaning I am likely out-kicked if my opponent also has a 9. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check to the raiser and see how they react to this wet texture. This allows me to control the pot size and evaluate their betting strength.

I hold middle pair (Eights) with a Jack kicker on a coordinated 6-8-9 board. The board texture connects very well with the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many straights (75, T7), two pairs, and sets, as well as numerous straight draws (any 7, 5, or T). Betting here exposes me to a check-raise which would force me to fold a hand with significant showdown value. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid difficult spots against check-raises, and look to call reasonable bets on safe turns or get to showdown cheaply. My hand is too strong to fold but marginal for three streets of value or sustaining heavy aggression.

The Turn card is an Ace, which is a classic 'scare card' that generally favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Since I checked the flop and Pro checked back, Pro could easily have an Ace that they were playing cautiously or a hand like a medium pocket pair. My pair of nines is now second pair with a very weak kicker. Leading into the Ace doesn't accomplish much, as I'll likely only be called by better hands (Aces, better nines, or straights) and fold out hands I currently beat. Checking is the best line to control the pot size and see how Pro reacts to the Ace.

I hold a pair of Eights which was middle pair on the flop but is now third pair after the Ace on the turn. The board (6c 8d 9h As) connects heavily with the Big Blind's range, which includes many straights, two pairs, and random Aces. Flash's check on the turn signals some weakness or a draw, but betting here provides little value; worse hands (like 6x or 77) might fold, and better hands (Ax, 9x) will call or raise. By checking back, I avoid being check-raised, control the size of the pot, and ensure I see the river to realize my showdown value cheaply.

The board has become extremely coordinated with 6-7-8-9-A, meaning any 5 or 10 makes a straight. My pair of nines is now quite weak given the potential straights and the Ace on the board. Pro checked back both the flop and the turn, which suggests a hand with some showdown value but not a monster, such as a medium pair or potentially a weak Ace. I have very little value in betting here, as I likely only get called by hands that beat me (straights, Aces, or better nines) and fold out hands I already beat. Checking is the best option to see a cheap showdown and minimize losses on a dangerous board.

The river creates a very dangerous board texture with a 4-card straight (6-7-8-9) present. Any 5 or Ten makes a straight. My hand is a pair of Eights, which is now 3rd pair on the board. The opponent has checked to me three times, signaling weakness or a trap. Betting here is not profitable: worse hands like high-card misses will fold, and better hands (Aces, Nines, or straights) will call or check-raise. I have enough showdown value to beat missed overcards (King/Queen high) or lower pairs, so the best play is to check back and get to showdown cheaply.

Ten-three offsuit is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation from the button. While I have the advantage of position, this specific hand has poor playability and very little equity. My opponent, Pro, has shown they are willing to 3-bet or play aggressively post-flop. Rather than risking chips on a weak opening or a limp-fold, I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 6d 5h, a weak off-suit connector. In Heads-Up play, while being in position is advantageous, 65o is statistically near the bottom 30-35% of hands and is typically a fold or a part of a limping range. My strategy so far has been Raise or Fold. Introducing a limp now might be suboptimal without a balanced range, and raising with this specific holding is too loose given it lacks high card strength and flush potential. Although the opponent has been folding to raises frequently, I will preserve my chip lead and fold this weak hand to maintain a stronger opening range.

Holding Qd 5d on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a hand that is well within the standard opening range. Q5 suited has decent equity and playability post-flop. I will raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot.

I am in the Big Blind with 5h 7h facing a 3x raise (300) from the Button. My hand is a suited one-gapper with decent playability and equity against a standard Button opening range. The pot odds are roughly 2:1, requiring 33% equity, which this hand generally satisfies or comes close to, especially considering implied odds at this stack depth (78 BB effective). Folding is too tight, and 3-betting is unnecessary without specific reads. Calling allows me to see a flop and evaluate.

I am out of position with middle pair (5s) on a monotone club board (3c 5c 9c). I have no clubs in my hand. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6), the flush potential on board makes this hand vulnerable. Leading out would likely only get action from better hands or flush draws that have decent equity against me. Checking is the standard line to the pre-flop raiser, allowing me to evaluate their action and potentially see a cheaper turn card.

I have middle pair (fives) on a very wet, monotone board (3c 5c 9c). While I have a piece of the board, I do not hold any clubs, making my hand extremely vulnerable to any club in the opponent's range. Checking back here controls the pot size and allows me to see a turn card for free, preserving my stack while maintaining some showdown value.

On the turn, the board is 3c 5c 9c 3s. I hold 5h 7h, giving me two pair (Threes and Fives) with a Nine kicker. My opponent checked back the monotone flop, which caps their range; they likely lack a flush, set, or strong top pair, as these hands usually bet for protection or value on such a dynamic board. The turn card pairs the board (3s), which reduces the likelihood of my hand being best if they held a 3, but given the flop check, a 3 is less likely unless it's something like A3 or 43. My hand has decent showdown value against Ace-high or small pocket pairs. Betting likely only gets called by better hands (9x, 66+, flushes) or strong draws, while folding out the bluffs I beat. Checking controls the pot size and allows me to induce bluffs or call a reasonable bet on the river. I will check to the raiser.

I have a pair of fives on a board with three clubs. The turn card, a three of spades, pairs the lowest card on the board. Since my opponent has checked for the second time, it is likely they either have a marginal hand or are playing a flush/stronger hand slowly. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I reach the river with my showdown value without being forced to face a check-raise on this monotone board.

On the river, the board is 3c 5c 9c 3s 5s. I hold 5h 7h, giving me a Full House (Fives full of Threes). This hand is incredibly strong, beating flushes, straights, and trip threes (3x makes Threes full of Fives). I only lose to 99 or 59, which are unlikely given the passive action, and I chop with any other 5x. The opponent checked back a monotone flop and a pairing turn, signaling a weak range likely consisting of marginal pairs or Ace-high. Checking is suboptimal because this passive opponent will likely check back to take showdown value. I need to bet to extract value from a 9, a 3, or a suspicious pocket pair. A small-to-medium bet mimics a missed draw bluff or a blocking bet, maximizing the chance of a call from weaker hands. I will bet 250 (slightly less than half pot).

I have a full house, fives full of threes (5-5-5-3-3), on a board of 3c-5c-9c-3s-5s. Pro leads for 250 after both players checked the flop and turn. This board is double-paired, meaning any 3 or 5 makes a full house. My full house is higher than any made with a 3 (3-3-3-5-5). I only lose to the improbable 99 or quads (33). This lead from Pro likely represents a 3, a flush, or a 9 trying to buy the pot or value bet thinly. I will raise to 800 to maximize value from these hands.

I hold a Full House (Fives full of Threes) on a double-paired board. The opponent checked back a monotone flop and checked the turn, which strongly discounts stronger hands like 99 or 95 that would typically bet for protection or value. The river raise suggests they have improved (likely holding a 3 or 5), have a flush, or are bluffing. Since I beat any 3x (Threes full of Fives) and flushes, and I chop with any 5x, I am very well positioned. Re-raising is risky as it likely folds out the worse hands I beat (like 3x or flushes) and isolates me against the few hands that beat me or chops. Calling is the highest EV play to secure the pot against their value range and catch bluffs.

In Heads-Up play, Queen-Seven suited is a strong starting hand from the Button (Small Blind). It falls well within the raising range, possessing both high card value and flush potential. Given the history where min-raising has been effective and allows me to play the pot in position, I will raise to 200 (2 BB) to pressure the Big Blind and build a pot with a playable hand.

I am in the Big Blind with T5s. Pro has raised to 200 from the Button. This is a standard min-raise. I need to call 100 more to see the flop, which gives me 3:1 pot odds (100 to win a total pot of 400). T5s is a suitable hand for defending the big blind against a button open given these odds. I will call and see a flop.

I have completely missed this flop (K-high, rainbow). I have Ten-high with no draws. The board favors the pre-flop aggressor's range significantly. My best course of action is to check and see what the opponent does, likely folding to any significant bet as I have no equity to continue.

The flop comes K-8-2 rainbow, a very dry board texture. As the pre-flop raiser, this board hits my range (which includes strong Kings and sets) better than the Big Blind's calling range. However, with Queen-high and a backdoor flush draw, my hand has marginal showdown value. Betting here is a viable option to fold out lower equity hands, but checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, control the size of the pot, and potentially induce bluffs on later streets or hit a Queen/Seven/Club on the turn. Given the opponent's capability to check-raise (seen in Hand #4) and the early stage of the match, taking the low-variance line in position is prudent.

On the turn, I have picked up a flush draw with the 6 of clubs. On the flop, both of us checked, which suggests the opponent likely doesn't have a King, or is playing it very cautiously. By checking again, I can potentially see the river for free to hit my flush or evaluate the situation if the opponent decides to bet. Since I am out of position and only have a draw, checking is a standard and safe play to realize my equity.

The turn card [6c] provides a Queen-high flush draw, adding significant equity to my hand. The opponent has checked twice, suggesting a weak range or potentially a trap. While a semi-bluff bet could fold out weak pairs or equity denial, checking back is a superior low-variance play in position. It guarantees I see the river card to try and hit my flush or top pair without risking a check-raise. If I hit on the river, my hand is disguised; if I miss, I kept the pot small.

I have Ten-high and missed my flush draw. The board is Jack-high after the river. Since both of us checked the flop and the turn, it's unlikely the opponent has a strong hand, but my Ten-high has almost no showdown value. However, a bluff here is unlikely to be successful given the small pot and the passive line taken by both players so far. I will check and fold if the opponent bets, as I cannot win the hand without a successful bluff and the board doesn't provide a strong story for one.

The opponent has checked to me on all three streets (Flop, Turn, River), signaling a weak range likely consisting of Ace-high, bottom pair, or missed draws. My Queen-high has very little showdown value and will likely lose to Ace-high or any pair. In Hand #3, I successfully utilized this exact line (Raise Pre, Check Flop, Check Turn, Bet River) to fold out the opponent in a similar spot. Since the board runs out K-8-2-6-J, a bet here credibly represents a King that was pot-controlling or a Jack that hit the river. A half-pot bet is sufficient to fold out better hands like Ace-high or weak pairs (2x, 6x, 8x) while risking a small portion of my stack.

I have Ten-high and missed my flush draw. After checking through the flop and the turn, the opponent has bet 220 into a 400 pot on the river. My Ten-high has no showdown value, and even many of the opponent's bluffs (like Ace-high or Queen-high) would beat my current hand. Given the passive line taken throughout the hand, I have no reason to believe a bluff-raise would be successful, and calling is mathematically losing as I am at the bottom of my range. I will fold.

In heads-up play, the Small Blind/Button has the advantage of position for the rest of the hand. While standard strategy often dictates a wide opening range from this position, 9-3 offsuit is near the bottom of any reasonable range. Given that my opponent (Pro) has shown the ability to 3-bet and play aggressively, I will choose to fold this hand to preserve my stack and wait for a more favorable starting hand rather than trying to steal the blinds with such weak equity.

I hold J4o on the Button (SB). While I have position post-flop, J4o is a very weak hand. My opponent has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind frequently (Hands 3, 9, 13), making a steal attempt less likely to succeed immediately. I have previously folded weak buttons like K2o and 65o (Hands 5, 11). Given the deep stacks and the poor playability of this hand, folding is the most disciplined and consistent action to minimize losses.

I am on the button with 3h 2d, which is a very weak starting hand. While it's a heads-up situation and I could potentially steal the blinds, the hand has almost no post-flop value. Given the stack sizes and the early stage of the tournament (Hand 16), there's no need to play trash hands out of position or in marginal situations. Folding is the most prudent play.

I hold 4d 2d on the Button (SB). This is a very weak suited 1-gapper. While I have position and a chip lead, my opponent has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind by calling raises (Hands 3, 9, 13). Since 42s has very low playability and equity when called, and I have previously folded hands like 65o and K2o from this position, maintaining a fold here is consistent and prudent to preserve my stack lead without taking unnecessary risks with the bottom of my range.

I'm holding Eight-Nine suited on the button. This is a very strong hand for heads-up play, offering both straight and flush potential. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to exert pressure on the Big Blind and take control of the pot early. Given Pro's tendency to defend but also occasionally fold to pre-flop aggression, building a pot with a hand that flops well is the optimal strategy.

I am in the Big Blind with Q6o facing a 3x raise (300 chips) from the Button (Flash). While Q6o has reasonable raw equity heads-up, it suffers from poor playability out of position, especially against a larger open size like 3bb. I need 33% equity to call based on pot odds, but realizing that equity OOP with a weak off-suit Queen is difficult. Flash has been opening to 300 consistently, and I have folded similar or slightly worse hands (87o, 95o) to this sizing previously. Preserving my chip lead (11950 vs 7650) by avoiding marginal negative EV spots OOP is the prudent strategy.

I hold Jack-Three suited (Jd 3d) on the button. Based on my previous play history in this session, I have adopted a strategy of folding the bottom portion of my range on the button, having previously folded hands like K2o (Hand #5), 52s (Hand #7), and J4o (Hand #15). J3s is comparable in strength to these folded hands and often dominated. Although I have a chip lead, the stacks are deep (approx 80BB effective), allowing me to wait for better spots to apply pressure rather than opening with marginal holdings that can lead to difficult post-flop situations. Therefore, maintaining consistency with my 'Raise or Fold' strategy where I fold weak hands, I will fold.

Holding pocket Threes (3d, 3h) on the button, I have a solid starting hand for a heads-up match. My strategy so far has involved opening for 3x the big blind (300 total) from this position, and I will maintain that consistency here. This raise allows me to take control of the hand, potentially end it pre-flop, or build a pot where I can set-mine or outplay my opponent on later streets.

Betting here risks bloating the pot against an Ace or facing a check-raise that forces me to fold my equity. It avoids isolating myself against a stronger range while my hand has decent showdown value.

On the River, the board is Ah 6h 2c Jc Qs. I hold 9h Jd, giving me third pair. The opponent has checked to me on all three streets (Flop, Turn, River). Betting here is thin; worse hands (like 6x or 2x) are likely to fold given the scary board (A, Q, J), and better hands (Ax, Qx, better Jx) will call. Therefore, betting accomplishes little value and risks reopening the action. My hand has sufficient showdown value to beat missed draws and lower pairs. Checking back is the optimal play to realize my equity and take the pot if my hand is good without risking more chips.

By checking, I keep his range wide and give him the opportunity to put more chips in the pot with bluffs or marginal hands. I intend to check-call or check-raise depending on his sizing, but starting with a check maximizes expected value.

On this dry, unconnected board of 7-9-3, my King-high still holds decent showdown value but is vulnerable to overcards or any piece of the board.
